title: Power / Thermal Guide
summary: Understand node count, watts per node, peak factor, overhead, rack impact, heat output, and operating margin for compute equipment.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates the electrical and heat impact of compute nodes so rack power, UPS load, and room cooling can be reviewed before the environment becomes the limit.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning servers, GPU nodes, racks, closets, UPS load, room cooling, or equipment expansion.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible Power & Thermal inputs and explain how each value affects electrical and cooling pressure.

items.label: Nodes
items.description: The number of compute nodes, servers, or equivalent pieces of equipment in the modeled group.
items.whyItMatters: Node count multiplies both power draw and heat output.
items.commonMistake: Checking one server and forgetting the full deployed count.

items.label: Watts per Node
items.description: The expected electrical load of each node under the modeled operating condition.
items.whyItMatters: Almost all IT electrical load becomes heat that the space must remove.
items.commonMistake: Using idle wattage when the system will run sustained production load.

items.label: Peak Factor
items.description: A multiplier for higher-than-normal load, boost behavior, workload bursts, or conservative planning.
items.whyItMatters: Peak factor prevents the design from being based only on calm average draw.
items.commonMistake: Assuming nameplate, idle, or one test run represents every operating state.

items.label: Overhead (%)
items.description: Extra allowance for power-supply loss, PDU/circuit planning, environmental inefficiency, accessories, or future growth.
items.whyItMatters: Overhead provides practical space between calculated load and real infrastructure limits.
items.commonMistake: Counting every amp of circuit capacity as usable continuous IT load.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means power and heat appear manageable. Watch means rack, circuit, UPS, or room cooling assumptions should be reviewed. Risk means the equipment may exceed practical power or cooling limits.
bullets: Power draw and heat output are linked; lowering one usually lowers the other.
Rack limits can be reached before room limits.
Redundant power should not automatically be counted as extra normal usable capacity.

sections.title: How this fits in the Compute flow
body: Power / Thermal takes the compute design and checks whether the surrounding environment can actually support it.
bullets: Use CPU, VM, and GPU results to keep power assumptions realistic.
Power and cooling pressure should be reviewed before final hardware selection.
If this step is tight, the design may need fewer nodes, lower-density hardware, better cooling, or a different rack plan.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most compute planning problems come from trusting one comfortable number while ignoring the rest of the platform. CPU, RAM, storage, network, power, cooling, protection timing, and growth all interact.
bullets: Sizing only for average load instead of peak behavior.
Ignoring headroom, background tasks, maintenance windows, and growth.
Treating theoretical maximums as sustained real-world capacity.
Forgetting that one weak resource can make the rest of the system feel undersized.
Failing to document assumptions before hardware, licensing, or architecture decisions are locked in.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, application requirements, benchmark testing, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications still matter.
Application behavior may change the final requirement.
Site conditions, operating procedures, and maintenance expectations may affect the design.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, application testing, compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, platform, and software being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
Use the results as decision support, not as a final authority.
